commit 7e94b6d9e008da4f75d6a61cc230735c03ded307
Author: Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>

mm/pagewalk: fix race between concurrent split and refault

This commit aims to fix a race in walk_pud_range() where a concurrent
thread refaults a PUD leaf entry. It validates the PUD entry in
walk_pmd_range() using a stable snapshot and retries via ACTION_AGAIN if
the PUD is not present or is a leaf.

> diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c
> index a94c401ab2cf..4e7bcd975c54 100644
> --- a/mm/pagewalk.c
> +++ b/mm/pagewalk.c
> @@ -97,6 +97,7 @@ static int walk_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  			  struct mm_walk *walk)
>  {
> +	pud_t pudval = pudp_get(pud);
>  	pmd_t *pmd;
>  	unsigned long next;

[ ... ]

> +	if (!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)) {
> +		walk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;
> +		return 0;
> +	}
> +
>  	pmd = pmd_offset(pud, addr);

Is there a race condition because pmd_offset() dereferences the pud pointer
instead of using the validated pudval snapshot?

If a concurrent thread modifies the PUD entry into a huge PUD leaf exactly
between the pud_leaf(pudval) check and the pmd_offset() call, couldn't
pmd_offset() read the newly modified entry from memory?

This might cause it to interpret a leaf PFN (like IO memory) as a PMD table
address, potentially resulting in the exact same crash this patch aims to fix.

[ ... ]

> @@ -218,12 +237,12 @@ static int walk_pud_range(p4d_t *p4d, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,

[ ... ]

>  		err = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);
>  		if (err)
>  			break;
> +
> +		if (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)
> +			goto again;
>  	} while (pud++, addr = next, addr != end);

Can this cause an infinite loop (livelock) when walking non-splittable PUD
leaves?

In walk_pud_range(), it attempts to split the PUD using split_huge_pud().
However, split_huge_pud() only operates on Transparent Huge Pages where
pud_trans_huge() is true.

For raw PFN-mapped device memory, like the VM_PFNMAP mappings used by large
VFIO-PCI BARs mentioned in the commit message, pud_trans_huge() is false.
Because split_huge_pud() safely does nothing, the PUD entry remains a
permanent leaf.

If walk_pmd_range() returns ACTION_AGAIN because it sees a PUD leaf, won't
this goto again jump back to the start of the loop and process the exact
same un-splittable PUD entry indefinitely without advancing the address?

commit 7e94b6d9e008da4f75d6a61cc230735c03ded307
Author: Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 25 09:59:16 2026 +0000

    mm/pagewalk: fix race between concurrent split and refault
    
    The splitting of a PUD entry in walk_pud_range() can race with
    a concurrent thread refaulting the PUD leaf entry causing it to
    try walking a PMD range that has disappeared.
    
    An example and reproduction of this is to try reading numa_maps of
    a process while VFIO-PCI is setting up DMA (specifically the
    vfio_pin_pages_remote call) on a large BAR for that process.
    
    This will trigger a kernel BUG:
    vfio-pci 0000:03:00.0: enabling device (0000 -> 0002)
    BUG: unable to handle page fault for address: ffffa23980000000
    PGD 0 P4D 0
    Oops: Oops: 0000 [#1] SMP NOPTI
    ...
    RIP: 0010:walk_pgd_range+0x3b5/0x7a0
    Code: 8d 43 ff 48 89 44 24 28 4d 89 ce 4d 8d a7 00 00 20 00 48 8b 4c 24
    28 49 81 e4 00 00 e0 ff 49 8d 44 24 ff 48 39 c8 4c 0f 43 e3 <49> f7 06
       9f ff ff ff 75 3b 48 8b 44 24 20 48 8b 40 28 48 85 c0 74
    RSP: 0018:ffffac23e1ecf808 EFLAGS: 00010287
    RAX: 00007f44c01fffff RBX: 00007f4500000000 RCX: 00007f44ffffffff
    RDX: 0000000000000000 RSI: 000ffffffffff000 RDI: ffffffff93378fe0

    R13: 00007f44c0000000 R14: ffffa23980000000 R15: 00007f44c0000000
    FS:  00007fe884739580(0000) GS:ffff9b7d7a9c0000(0000)
    knlGS:0000000000000000
    CS:  0010 DS: 0000 ES: 0000 CR0: 0000000080050033
    CR2: ffffa23980000000 CR3: 000000c0650e2005 CR4: 0000000000770ef0
    PKRU: 55555554
    Call Trace:
     <TASK>
     __walk_page_range+0x195/0x1b0
     walk_page_vma+0x62/0xc0
     show_numa_map+0x12b/0x3b0
     seq_read_iter+0x297/0x440
     seq_read+0x11d/0x140
     vfs_read+0xc2/0x340
     ksys_read+0x5f/0xe0
     do_syscall_64+0x68/0x130
     ? get_page_from_freelist+0x5c2/0x17e0
     ? mas_store_prealloc+0x17e/0x360
     ? vma_set_page_prot+0x4c/0xa0
     ? __alloc_pages_noprof+0x14e/0x2d0
     ? __mod_memcg_lruvec_state+0x8d/0x140
     ? __lruvec_stat_mod_folio+0x76/0xb0
     ? __folio_mod_stat+0x26/0x80
     ? do_anonymous_page+0x705/0x900
     ? __handle_mm_fault+0xa8d/0x1000
     ? __count_memcg_events+0x53/0xf0
     ? handle_mm_fault+0xa5/0x360
     ? do_user_addr_fault+0x342/0x640
     ? arch_exit_to_user_mode_prepare.constprop.0+0x16/0xa0
     ? irqentry_exit_to_user_mode+0x24/0x100
     entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x76/0x7e
    RIP: 0033:0x7fe88464f47e
    Code: c0 e9 b6 fe ff ff 50 48 8d 3d be 07 0b 00 e8 69 01 02 00 66 0f 1f
    84 00 00 00 00 00 64 8b 04 25 18 00 00 00 85 c0 75 14 0f 05 <48> 3d 00
       f0 ff ff 77 5a c3 66 0f 1f 84 00 00 00 00 00 48 83 ec 28
    RSP: 002b:00007ffe6cd9a9b8 EFLAGS: 00000246 ORIG_RAX: 0000000000000000
    RAX: ffffffffffffffda RBX: 0000000000020000 RCX: 00007fe88464f47e
    RDX: 0000000000020000 RSI: 00007fe884543000 RDI: 0000000000000003
    RBP: 00007fe884543000 R08: 00007fe884542010 R09: 0000000000000000
    R10: fffffffffffffbc5 R11: 0000000000000246 R12: 0000000000000000
    R13: 0000000000000003 R14: 0000000000020000 R15: 0000000000020000
     </TASK>
    
    Fix this by validating the PUD entry in walk_pmd_range() using a stable
    snapshot (pudp_get()). If the PUD is not present or is a leaf, retry the
    walk via ACTION_AGAIN instead of descending further. This mirrors the
    retry logic in walk_pte_range(), which lets walk_pmd_range() retry if
    the PTE is not being got by pte_offset_map_lock().
    
    Fixes: f9e54c3a2f5b ("vfio/pci: implement huge_fault support")
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
    Co-developed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>

diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c
index a94c401ab2cf..4e7bcd975c54 100644
--- a/mm/pagewalk.c
+++ b/mm/pagewalk.c
@@ -97,6 +97,7 @@ static int walk_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
 static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
 			  struct mm_walk *walk)
 {
+	pud_t pudval = pudp_get(pud);
 	pmd_t *pmd;
 	unsigned long next;
 	const struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;
@@ -105,6 +106,24 @@ static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
 	int err = 0;
 	int depth = real_depth(3);
 
+	/*
+	 * For PTE handling, pte_offset_map_lock() takes care of checking
+	 * whether there actually is a page table. But it also has to be
+	 * very careful about concurrent page table reclaim.
+	 *
+	 * Similarly, we have to be careful here - a PUD entry that points
+	 * to a PMD table cannot go away, so we can just walk it. But if
+	 * it's something else, we need to ensure we didn't race something,
+	 * so need to retry.
+	 *
+	 * A pertinent example of this is a PUD refault after PUD split -
+	 * we will need to split again or risk accessing invalid memory.
+	 */
+	if (!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)) {
+		walk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;
+		return 0;
+	}
+
 	pmd = pmd_offset(pud, addr);
 	do {
 again:
@@ -218,12 +237,12 @@ static int walk_pud_range(p4d_t *p4d, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
 		else if (pud_leaf(*pud) || !pud_present(*pud))
 			continue; /* Nothing to do. */
 
-		if (pud_none(*pud))
-			goto again;
-
 		err = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);
 		if (err)
 			break;
+
+		if (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)
+			goto again;
 	} while (pud++, addr = next, addr != end);
 
 	return err;

The original logic in `walk_pud_range()` has a `goto again` label, and it *seems* like setting `ACTION_AGAIN` triggers the goto, which re-checks the PUD.

However, I’m concerned by the *infinite loop* risk if these transient conditions are not handled properly. If the PUD constantly remains invalid or a leaf, the walker will loop indefinitely.

    "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0\n#include <linux/pagewalk.h>\n#include <linux/highmem.h>\n#include <linux/sched.h>\n#include <linux/hugetlb.h>\n#include <linux/mmu_context.h>\n#include <linux/swap.h>\n#include <linux/leafops.h>\n\n#include <asm/tlbflush.h>\n\n#include \"internal.h\"\n\n/*\n * We want to know the real level where a entry is located ignoring any\n * folding of levels which may be happening. For example if p4d is folded then\n * a missing entry found at level 1 (p4d) is actually at level 0 (pgd).\n */\nstatic int real_depth(int depth)\n{\n\tif (depth == 3 && PTRS_PER_PMD == 1)\n\t\tdepth = 2;\n\tif (depth == 2 && PTRS_PER_PUD == 1)\n\t\tdepth = 1;\n\tif (depth == 1 && PTRS_PER_P4D == 1)\n\t\tdepth = 0;\n\treturn depth;\n}\n\nstatic int walk_pte_range_inner(pte_t *pte, unsigned long addr,\n\t\t\t\tunsigned long end, struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tint err = 0;\n\n\tfor (;;) {\n\t\tif (ops->install_pte && pte_none(ptep_get(pte))) {\n\t\t\tpte_t new_pte;\n\n\t\t\terr = ops->install_pte(addr, addr + PAGE_SIZE, &new_pte,\n\t\t\t\t\t       walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\t\tset_pte_at(walk->mm, addr, pte, new_pte);\n\t\t\t/* Non-present before, so for arches that need it. */\n\t\t\tif (!WARN_ON_ONCE(walk->no_vma))\n\t\t\t\tupdate_mmu_cache(walk->vma, addr, pte);\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\terr = ops->pte_entry(pte, addr, addr + PAGE_SIZE, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\t\tif (addr >= end - PAGE_SIZE)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\taddr += PAGE_SIZE;\n\t\tpte++;\n\t}\n\treturn err;\n}\n\nstatic int walk_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t  struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tpte_t *pte;\n\tint err = 0;\n\tspinlock_t *ptl;\n\n\tif (walk->no_vma) {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * pte_offset_map() might apply user-specific validation.\n\t\t * Indeed, on x86_64 the pmd entries set up by init_espfix_ap()\n\t\t * fit its pmd_bad() check (_PAGE_NX set and _PAGE_RW clear),\n\t\t * and CONFIG_EFI_PGT_DUMP efi_mm goes so far as to walk them.\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (walk->mm == &init_mm || addr >= TASK_SIZE)\n\t\t\tpte = pte_offset_kernel(pmd, addr);\n\t\telse\n\t\t\tpte = pte_offset_map(pmd, addr);\n\t\tif (pte) {\n\t\t\terr = walk_pte_range_inner(pte, addr, end, walk);\n\t\t\tif (walk->mm != &init_mm && addr < TASK_SIZE)\n\t\t\t\tpte_unmap(pte);\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\tpte = pte_offset_map_lock(walk->mm, pmd, addr, &ptl);\n\t\tif (pte) {\n\t\t\terr = walk_pte_range_inner(pte, addr, end, walk);\n\t\t\tpte_unmap_unlock(pte, ptl);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\tif (!pte)\n\t\twalk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;\n\treturn err;\n}\n\nstatic int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t  struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tpud_t pudval = pudp_get(pud);\n\tpmd_t *pmd;\n\tunsigned long next;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tbool has_handler = ops->pte_entry;\n\tbool has_install = ops->install_pte;\n\tint err = 0;\n\tint depth = real_depth(3);\n\n\t/*\n\t * For PTE handling, pte_offset_map_lock() takes care of checking\n\t * whether there actually is a page table. But it also has to be\n\t * very careful about concurrent page table reclaim.\n\t *\n\t * Similarly, we have to be careful here - a PUD entry that points\n\t * to a PMD table cannot go away, so we can just walk it. But if\n\t * it's something else, we need to ensure we didn't race something,\n\t * so need to retry.\n\t *\n\t * A pertinent example of this is a PUD refault after PUD split -\n\t * we will need to split again or risk accessing invalid memory.\n\t */\n\tif (!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)) {\n\t\twalk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\tpmd = pmd_offset(pud, addr);\n\tdo {\nagain:\n\t\tnext = pmd_addr_end(addr, end);\n\t\tif (pmd_none(*pmd)) {\n\t\t\tif (has_install)\n\t\t\t\terr = __pte_alloc(walk->mm, pmd);\n\t\t\telse if (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(addr, next, depth, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\tif (!has_install)\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\twalk->action = ACTION_SUBTREE;\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * This implies that each ->pmd_entry() handler\n\t\t * needs to know about pmd_trans_huge() pmds\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (ops->pmd_entry)\n\t\t\terr = ops->pmd_entry(pmd, addr, next, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\tif (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)\n\t\t\tgoto again;\n\t\tif (walk->action == ACTION_CONTINUE)\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\tif (!has_handler) { /* No handlers for lower page tables. */\n\t\t\tif (!has_install)\n\t\t\t\tcontinue; /* Nothing to do. */\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * We are ONLY installing, so avoid unnecessarily\n\t\t\t * splitting a present huge page.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tif (pmd_present(*pmd) && pmd_trans_huge(*pmd))\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif (walk->vma)\n\t\t\tsplit_huge_pmd(walk->vma, pmd, addr);\n\t\telse if (pmd_leaf(*pmd) || !pmd_present(*pmd))\n\t\t\tcontinue; /* Nothing to do. */\n\n\t\terr = walk_pte_range(pmd, addr, next, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\tif (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)\n\t\t\tgoto again;\n\n\t} while (pmd++, addr = next, addr != end);\n\n\treturn err;\n}\n\nstatic int walk_pud_range(p4d_t *p4d, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t  struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tpud_t *pud;\n\tunsigned long next;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tbool has_handler = ops->pmd_entry || ops->pte_entry;\n\tbool has_install = ops->install_pte;\n\tint err = 0;\n\tint depth = real_depth(2);\n\n\tpud = pud_offset(p4d, addr);\n\tdo {\n again:\n\t\tnext = pud_addr_end(addr, end);\n\t\tif (pud_none(*pud)) {\n\t\t\tif (has_install)\n\t\t\t\terr = __pmd_alloc(walk->mm, pud, addr);\n\t\t\telse if (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(addr, next, depth, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\tif (!has_install)\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\twalk->action = ACTION_SUBTREE;\n\n\t\tif (ops->pud_entry)\n\t\t\terr = ops->pud_entry(pud, addr, next, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\tif (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)\n\t\t\tgoto again;\n\t\tif (walk->action == ACTION_CONTINUE)\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\tif (!has_handler) { /* No handlers for lower page tables. */\n\t\t\tif (!has_install)\n\t\t\t\tcontinue; /* Nothing to do. */\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * We are ONLY installing, so avoid unnecessarily\n\t\t\t * splitting a present huge page.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tif (pud_present(*pud) && pud_trans_huge(*pud))\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif (walk->vma)\n\t\t\tsplit_huge_pud(walk->vma, pud, addr);\n\t\telse if (pud_leaf(*pud) || !pud_present(*pud))\n\t\t\tcontinue; /* Nothing to do. */\n\n\t\terr = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\tif (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)\n\t\t\tgoto again;\n\t} while (pud++, addr = next, addr != end);\n\n\treturn err;\n}\n\nstatic int walk_p4d_range(pgd_t *pgd, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t  struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tp4d_t *p4d;\n\tunsigned long next;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tbool has_handler = ops->pud_entry || ops->pmd_entry || ops->pte_entry;\n\tbool has_install = ops->install_pte;\n\tint err = 0;\n\tint depth = real_depth(1);\n\n\tp4d = p4d_offset(pgd, addr);\n\tdo {\n\t\tnext = p4d_addr_end(addr, end);\n\t\tif (p4d_none_or_clear_bad(p4d)) {\n\t\t\tif (has_install)\n\t\t\t\terr = __pud_alloc(walk->mm, p4d, addr);\n\t\t\telse if (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(addr, next, depth, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\tif (!has_install)\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\t\tif (ops->p4d_entry) {\n\t\t\terr = ops->p4d_entry(p4d, addr, next, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\t\tif (has_handler || has_install)\n\t\t\terr = walk_pud_range(p4d, addr, next, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t} while (p4d++, addr = next, addr != end);\n\n\treturn err;\n}\n\nstatic int walk_pgd_range(unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t  struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tpgd_t *pgd;\n\tunsigned long next;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tbool has_handler = ops->p4d_entry || ops->pud_entry || ops->pmd_entry ||\n\t\tops->pte_entry;\n\tbool has_install = ops->install_pte;\n\tint err = 0;\n\n\tif (walk->pgd)\n\t\tpgd = walk->pgd + pgd_index(addr);\n\telse\n\t\tpgd = pgd_offset(walk->mm, addr);\n\tdo {\n\t\tnext = pgd_addr_end(addr, end);\n\t\tif (pgd_none_or_clear_bad(pgd)) {\n\t\t\tif (has_install)\n\t\t\t\terr = __p4d_alloc(walk->mm, pgd, addr);\n\t\t\telse if (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(addr, next, 0, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\tif (!has_install)\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\t\tif (ops->pgd_entry) {\n\t\t\terr = ops->pgd_entry(pgd, addr, next, walk);\n\t\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\t\tif (has_handler || has_install)\n\t\t\terr = walk_p4d_range(pgd, addr, next, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t} while (pgd++, addr = next, addr != end);\n\n\treturn err;\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE\nstatic unsigned long hugetlb_entry_end(struct hstate *h, unsigned long addr,\n\t\t\t\t       unsigned long end)\n{\n\tunsigned long boundary = (addr & huge_page_mask(h)) + huge_page_size(h);\n\n\treturn min(boundary, end);\n}\n\nstatic int walk_hugetlb_range(unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t      struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->vma;\n\tstruct hstate *h = hstate_vma(vma);\n\tunsigned long next;\n\tunsigned long hmask = huge_page_mask(h);\n\tunsigned long sz = huge_page_size(h);\n\tpte_t *pte;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tint err = 0;\n\n\thugetlb_vma_lock_read(vma);\n\tdo {\n\t\tnext = hugetlb_entry_end(h, addr, end);\n\t\tpte = hugetlb_walk(vma, addr & hmask, sz);\n\t\tif (pte)\n\t\t\terr = ops->hugetlb_entry(pte, hmask, addr, next, walk);\n\t\telse if (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(addr, next, -1, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t} while (addr = next, addr != end);\n\thugetlb_vma_unlock_read(vma);\n\n\treturn err;\n}\n\n#else /* CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE */\nstatic int walk_hugetlb_range(unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t      struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\n#endif /* CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE */\n\n/*\n * Decide whether we really walk over the current vma on [@start, @end)\n * or skip it via the returned value. Return 0 if we do walk over the\n * current vma, and return 1 if we skip the vma. Negative values means\n * error, where we abort the current walk.\n */\nstatic int walk_page_test(unsigned long start, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\tstruct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->vma;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\n\tif (ops->test_walk)\n\t\treturn ops->test_walk(start, end, walk);\n\n\t/*\n\t * vma(VM_PFNMAP) doesn't have any valid struct pages behind VM_PFNMAP\n\t * range, so we don't walk over it as we do for normal vmas. However,\n\t * Some callers are interested in handling hole range and they don't\n\t * want to just ignore any single address range. Such users certainly\n\t * define their ->pte_hole() callbacks, so let's delegate them to handle\n\t * vma(VM_PFNMAP).\n\t */\n\tif (vma->vm_flags & VM_PFNMAP) {\n\t\tint err = 1;\n\t\tif (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(start, end, -1, walk);\n\t\treturn err ? err : 1;\n\t}\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic int __walk_page_range(unsigned long start, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\tstruct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tint err = 0;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->vma;\n\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops = walk->ops;\n\tbool is_hugetlb = is_vm_hugetlb_page(vma);\n\n\t/* We do not support hugetlb PTE installation. */\n\tif (ops->install_pte && is_hugetlb)\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\tif (ops->pre_vma) {\n\t\terr = ops->pre_vma(start, end, walk);\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\treturn err;\n\t}\n\n\tif (is_hugetlb) {\n\t\tif (ops->hugetlb_entry)\n\t\t\terr = walk_hugetlb_range(start, end, walk);\n\t} else\n\t\terr = walk_pgd_range(start, end, walk);\n\n\tif (ops->post_vma)\n\t\tops->post_vma(walk);\n\n\treturn err;\n}\n\nstatic inline void process_mm_walk_lock(struct mm_struct *mm,\n\t\t\t\t\tenum page_walk_lock walk_lock)\n{\n\tif (walk_lock == PGWALK_RDLOCK)\n\t\tmmap_assert_locked(mm);\n\telse if (walk_lock != PGWALK_VMA_RDLOCK_VERIFY)\n\t\tmmap_assert_write_locked(mm);\n}\n\nstatic inline void process_vma_walk_lock(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t\t enum page_walk_lock walk_lock)\n{\n#ifdef CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK\n\tswitch (walk_lock) {\n\tcase PGWALK_WRLOCK:\n\t\tvma_start_write(vma);\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase PGWALK_WRLOCK_VERIFY:\n\t\tvma_assert_write_locked(vma);\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase PGWALK_VMA_RDLOCK_VERIFY:\n\t\tvma_assert_locked(vma);\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase PGWALK_RDLOCK:\n\t\t/* PGWALK_RDLOCK is handled by process_mm_walk_lock */\n\t\tbreak;\n\t}\n#endif\n}\n\n/*\n * See the comment for walk_page_range(), this performs the heavy lifting of the\n * operation, only sets no restrictions on how the walk proceeds.\n *\n * We usually restrict the ability to install PTEs, but this functionality is\n * available to internal memory management code and provided in mm/internal.h.\n */\nint walk_page_range_mm_unsafe(struct mm_struct *mm, unsigned long start,\n\t\tunsigned long end, const struct mm_walk_ops *ops,\n\t\tvoid *private)\n{\n\tint err = 0;\n\tunsigned long next;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma;\n\tstruct mm_walk walk = {\n\t\t.ops\t\t= ops,\n\t\t.mm\t\t= mm,\n\t\t.private\t= private,\n\t};\n\n\tif (start >= end)\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\tif (!walk.mm)\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\tprocess_mm_walk_lock(walk.mm, ops->walk_lock);\n\n\tvma = find_vma(walk.mm, start);\n\tdo {\n\t\tif (!vma) { /* after the last vma */\n\t\t\twalk.vma = NULL;\n\t\t\tnext = end;\n\t\t\tif (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(start, next, -1, &walk);\n\t\t} else if (start < vma->vm_start) { /* outside vma */\n\t\t\twalk.vma = NULL;\n\t\t\tnext = min(end, vma->vm_start);\n\t\t\tif (ops->pte_hole)\n\t\t\t\terr = ops->pte_hole(start, next, -1, &walk);\n\t\t} else { /* inside vma */\n\t\t\tprocess_vma_walk_lock(vma, ops->walk_lock);\n\t\t\twalk.vma = vma;\n\t\t\tnext = min(end, vma->vm_end);\n\t\t\tvma = find_vma(mm, vma->vm_end);\n\n\t\t\terr = walk_page_test(start, next, &walk);\n\t\t\tif (err > 0) {\n\t\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t\t * positive return values are purely for\n\t\t\t\t * controlling the pagewalk, so should never\n\t\t\t\t * be passed to the callers.\n\t\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t\terr = 0;\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\tif (err < 0)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\terr = __walk_page_range(start, next, &walk);\n\t\t}\n\t\tif (err)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t} while (start = next, start < end);\n\treturn err;\n}\n\n/*\n * Determine if the walk operations specified are permitted to be used for a\n * page table walk.\n *\n * This check is performed on all functions which are parameterised by walk\n * operations and exposed in include/linux/pagewalk.h.\n *\n * Internal memory management code can use *_unsafe() functions to be able to\n * use all page walking operations.\n */\nstatic bool check_ops_safe(const struct mm_walk_ops *ops)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * The installation of PTEs is solely under the control of memory\n\t * management logic and subject to many subtle locking, security and\n\t * cache considerations so we cannot permit other users to do so, and\n\t * certainly not for exported symbols.\n\t */\n\tif (ops->install_pte)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\treturn true;\n}\n\n/**\n * walk_page_range - walk page table with caller specific callbacks\n * @mm:\t\tmm_struct representing the target process of page table walk\n * @start:\tstart address of the virtual address range\n * @end:\tend address of the virtual address range\n * @ops:\toperation to call during the walk\n * @private:\tprivate data for callbacks' usage\n *\n * Recursively walk the page table tree of the process represented by @mm\n * within the virtual address range [@start, @end). During walking, we can do\n * some caller-specific works for each entry, by setting up pmd_entry(),\n * pte_entry(), and/or hugetlb_entry(). If you don't set up for some of these\n * callbacks, the associated entries/pages are just ignored.\n * The return values of these callbacks are commonly defined like below:\n *\n *  - 0  : succeeded to handle the current entry, and if you don't reach the\n *         end address yet, continue to walk.\n *  - >0 : succeeded to handle the current entry, and return to the caller\n *         with caller specific value.\n *  - <0 : failed to handle the current entry, and return to the caller\n *         with error code.\n *\n * Before starting to walk page table, some callers want to check whether\n * they really want to walk over the current vma, typically by checking\n * its vm_flags. walk_page_test() and @ops->test_walk() are used for this\n * purpose.\n *\n * If operations need to be staged before and committed after a vma is walked,\n * there are two callbacks, pre_vma() and post_vma(). Note that post_vma(),\n * since it is intended to handle commit-type operations, can't return any\n * errors.\n *\n * struct mm_walk keeps current values of some common data like vma and pmd,\n * which are useful for the access from callbacks. If you want to pass some\n * caller-specific data to callbacks, @private should be helpful.\n *\n * Locking:\n *   Callers of walk_page_range() and walk_page_vma() should hold @mm->mmap_lock,\n *   because these function traverse vma list and/or access to vma's data.\n */\nint walk_page_range(struct mm_struct *mm, unsigned long start,\n\t\tunsigned long end, const struct mm_walk_ops *ops,\n\t\tvoid *private)\n{\n\tif (!check_ops_safe(ops))\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\treturn walk_page_range_mm_unsafe(mm, start, end, ops, private);\n}\n\n/**\n * walk_kernel_page_table_range - walk a range of kernel pagetables.\n * @start:\tstart address of the virtual address range\n * @end:\tend address of the virtual address range\n * @ops:\toperation to call during the walk\n * @pgd:\tpgd to walk if different from mm->pgd\n * @private:\tprivate data for callbacks' usage\n *\n * Similar to walk_page_range() but can walk any page tables even if they are\n * not backed by VMAs. Because 'unusual' entries may be walked this function\n * will also not lock the PTEs for the pte_entry() callback. This is useful for\n * walking kernel pages tables or page tables for firmware.\n *\n * Note: Be careful to walk the kernel pages tables, the caller may be need to\n * take other effective approaches (mmap lock may be insufficient) to prevent\n * the intermediate kernel page tables belonging to the specified address range\n * from being freed (e.g. memory hot-remove).\n */\nint walk_kernel_page_table_range(unsigned long start, unsigned long end,\n\t\tconst struct mm_walk_ops *ops, pgd_t *pgd, void *private)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * Kernel intermediate page tables are usually not freed, so the mmap\n\t * read lock is sufficient. But there are some exceptions.\n\t * E.g. memory hot-remove. Okay, so I've been digging into the page table walking code, specifically looking at potential issues with migration entries and other non-standard PMD/PUD entries, and it looks like I've found a couple of interesting scenarios. Initially, I saw a potential infinite loop in `walk_pmd_range` if a PMD was a migration entry, but thankfully, `split_huge_pmd` handles that case by splitting the migration entry into PTE-level entries. Specifically, the function `pmd_is_huge` recognizes migration entries, and `__split_huge_pmd` correctly converts them, so that's not a concern. That's a relief!

However, the PUD situation is different and a bit more concerning. In `walk_pud_range`, `split_huge_pud` is only called if `pud_trans_huge` is true. This function does *not* split PUD migration entries (because they don't exist!). But then `walk_pmd_range` is called unconditionally on this entry and ends up looping forever if `pud_leaf` returns true. If it were a PUD migration entry, then it would not loop! But it would be very interesting if it was a devmap entry because it might loop forever if `pud_trans_huge` is false. This needs some further investigation. If `pud_trans_huge` is false for this entry, `split_huge_pud` would do nothing. So `walk_pmd_range` is called, and `pud_leaf` would be true. `walk->action` is set to `ACTION_AGAIN`. Then it loops and goes to `ACTION_SUBTREE`. This will loop forever!

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Page Table Walk Bug and Its Fix**

Okay, so I've been diving deep into this page table walk issue, specifically the fix that was implemented. It seems the core problem arises when a PUD entry, which is initially a leaf (representing a huge page), gets split concurrently by another thread while a page table walker is traversing it. The old code had a race condition where the walker could dereference an invalid pointer, leading to a crash.

Here's my understanding:

*   **The Scenario:** A PUD entry can be a leaf, either a THP or a DAX entry. A concurrent thread can *split* this entry, essentially unmapping it. This is where the race begins.
*   **The Old Code's Flaw:** The walker might read the PUD entry, find it present and a leaf, and then try to walk its PMD range, but in the meantime, the other thread has already split the PUD, which clears it. This means the PMD pointer it's using points to garbage. This resulted in a kernel BUG as shown in the commit message.
*   **What `split_huge_pud` Does:** In the case where `vma` is not NULL and the PUD entry *is* a THP/DAX leaf, `split_huge_pud` gets called. Critically, on DAX VMs at least, it *unmaps* the huge page by clearing the PUD. It *doesn't* populate the PMD table; the next page fault will trigger that. If it *fails* to clear the PUD due to a check such as `pud_trans_huge` being false, then an infinite loop occurs.
*   **The Race and the Fix:** The key fix is in `walk_pmd_range` where it checks `!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)`. if these are true, it returns `ACTION_AGAIN` with the loop going to the `again` label. This protects against the race. This correctly identifies the refault and avoids the bad dereference. The code now correctly handles it by seeing that the PUD is now `pud_none` and skipping the entry by jumping to the next PUD.
*   **The Important Distinction:** The code that uses `ops->pte_hole()` means that if the PUD is now empty (because of the split), it doesn't try to walk any PMDs. Instead, it calls the `pte_hole` function, which, I presume, handles the case of "missing" page table entries. The `continue` statement makes sure the code skips that PUD.
*   **Infinite Loop Issue:** There *is* an infinite loop if `split_huge_pud` returns without actually clearing the PUD. That can occur on a few different architectures. For instance on ARM64, `pud_trans_huge` can be false on a `pud_leaf`. In these cases, the code keeps looping.
*   **Cases Where This Doesn't Happen:** If `walk->vma` is NULL, then it skips the call to `split_huge_pud`, which is good. If `split_huge_pud` *succeeds* in clearing the PUD, the code correctly calls the `pte_hole` handler and advances.

So, in summary, the fix cleverly detects and handles the concurrent PUD splitting, preventing the kernel from dereferencing an invalid pointer. The fix also handles the corner case where the PUD is cleared and properly calls the `pte_hole` handler.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Infinite Loop in PUD Handling on PowerPC Hash MMU with DAX**

Okay, so I'm thinking through this PUD handling, specifically related to DAX mappings. Initially, I thought everything was fine, especially considering the absence of swap entries at the PUD level. The logic seemed to cover cases where a PUD might be cleared but not fully, or if it was a devmap entry. The `walk_pmd_range` and `walk_pud_range` functions, along with `split_huge_pud`, seemed to converge on the correct path, clearing the PUD down to a `pud_none` state eventually, and then moving on. Even when `pud_trans_huge` was true (like on x86 for DAX VMAs), the splitting mechanism looked correct.

However, the architecture-specific aspects are always where it gets interesting, so I started digging into how `pud_trans_huge` and `pud_leaf` are defined on other architectures. ARM64 looks functionally equivalent, but PowerPC on a Hash MMU is where a crucial difference appears.

On PowerPC, with the Hash MMU enabled, `pud_trans_huge` can be zero while `pud_leaf` can still be true if the `_PAGE_HUGE` flag is set. This immediately triggers alarm bells. If `pud_leaf` is true, but `pud_trans_huge` is false, `walk_pmd_range` leads to an `ACTION_AGAIN`, which brings us back to `split_huge_pud`.

Here's the problem: when `pud_trans_huge` is zero, `split_huge_pud` *doesn't* split the PUD. It sees a "non-trans_huge" PUD, and does nothing! Then, the entire process loops back to the beginning, resulting in an infinite loop!

I need to confirm this carefully, particularly whether a VMA *can* have a PUD where `_PAGE_HUGE` is set but `pud_trans_huge` is zero on the PowerPC Hash MMU. This would require the PUD to be marked as a huge page but not be treated as a trans-huge page by the kernel, something that is only relevant for non THP usage of large pages.

So, if Hash MMU *does* support 1GB DAX mappings, which makes `pud_leaf` true but `pud_trans_huge` false, then `split_huge_pud` will fail to split it, leading to the infinite loop. The critical question now: **does the PowerPC Hash MMU support 1GB DAX mappings?** That's the key to understanding the potential for this infinite loop!

The patch misdiagnosed an unsupported stable PUD state as a transient race condition, effectively replacing a page fault crash with a guaranteed CPU hard lockup (Denial of Service) for un-splittable PUDs."},{"problem":"Incomplete fix (TOCTOU race) because `pmd_offset()` dereferences the live PUD pointer instead of using the validated snapshot.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. The patch attempts to fix a concurrent modification race by reading a stable snapshot of the PUD entry (`pudval = pudp_get(pud)`) and verifying it is safe to traverse (`!pud_leaf(pudval)`).
2. However, immediately after this check, it calls `pmd_offset(pud, addr)` to calculate the PMD array pointer.
3. On many architectures (such as x86_64), `pmd_offset()` is implemented as a macro or inline function that directly dereferences the `pud` pointer argument again (e.g., computing `pud_page_vaddr(*pud)`).
4. If a concurrent thread modifies the PUD entry (e.g., refaults it into a huge PUD leaf) in the narrow window exactly between the `pud_leaf(pudval)` check and the `pmd_offset()` dereference, `pmd_offset()` will read the newly modified entry from memory.

Author: Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>

mm/pagewalk: fix race between concurrent split and refault

This commit aims to fix a race in walk_pud_range() where a concurrent
thread refaults a PUD leaf entry. It validates the PUD entry in
walk_pmd_range() using a stable snapshot and retries via ACTION_AGAIN if
the PUD is not present or is a leaf.

> diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c
> index a94c401ab2cf..4e7bcd975c54 100644
> --- a/mm/pagewalk.c
> +++ b/mm/pagewalk.c
> @@ -97,6 +97,7 @@ static int walk_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  			  struct mm_walk *walk)
>  {
> +	pud_t pudval = pudp_get(pud);
>  	pmd_t *pmd;
>  	unsigned long next;

[ ... ]

> +	if (!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)) {
> +		walk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;
> +		return 0;
> +	}
> +
>  	pmd = pmd_offset(pud, addr);

Is there a race condition because pmd_offset() dereferences the pud pointer
instead of using the validated pudval snapshot?

If a concurrent thread modifies the PUD entry into a huge PUD leaf exactly
between the pud_leaf(pudval) check and the pmd_offset() call, couldn't
pmd_offset() read the newly modified entry from memory?

This might cause it to interpret a leaf PFN (like IO memory) as a PMD table
address, potentially resulting in the exact same crash this patch aims to fix.

[ ... ]

> @@ -218,12 +237,12 @@ static int walk_pud_range(p4d_t *p4d, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,

[ ... ]

>  		err = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);
>  		if (err)
>  			break;
> +
> +		if (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)
> +			goto again;
>  	} while (pud++, addr = next, addr != end);

Can this cause an infinite loop (livelock) when walking non-splittable PUD
leaves?

In walk_pud_range(), it attempts to split the PUD using split_huge_pud().
However, split_huge_pud() only operates on Transparent Huge Pages where
pud_trans_huge() is true.

For raw PFN-mapped device memory, like the VM_PFNMAP mappings used by large
VFIO-PCI BARs mentioned in the commit message, pud_trans_huge() is false.
Because split_huge_pud() safely does nothing, the PUD entry remains a
permanent leaf.

If walk_pmd_range() returns ACTION_AGAIN because it sees a PUD leaf, won't
this goto again jump back to the start of the loop and process the exact
same un-splittable PUD entry indefinitely without advancing the address?
